three_body.instability_note: ESG score (54/100) flatters significantly vs sector peer rank (0/100) — a 54-point spread. No stable orbit: ESG scores are contaminated by peer-group construction and the FTSE 100's anti-ESG benchmark composition.
three_body.resolution: Our resolution: Integrity 37/100 × WEM 39/100 → 'Greenwashing Risk / Laggard'. Jangani, Date & Tucker (SSRN 5618192, 2026) show driver rankings shift across regime windows (COVID → COP26 → energy crisis). We anchor to verifiable harm metrics (emissions, pay ratios, enforcement records) that are regime-stable — not ESG labels that oscillate with market narrative.

summary_investor: Nestlé S.A. is projected to hold steady in its consumer peer ranking (2→2 of 2) over 3 months. FTSE 100 position: 29th→26th percentile. WEM 39/100 — material ESG risk — recommend underweight or significant risk premium. 4 high-severity incidents (36mo) represent unpriced tail risk. Placebo Index 0.85 — ESG narrative overstates real performance.
summary_corporate: 12-month sector ranking forecast: hold steady (2→2 of 2). FTSE 100: 29th→22nd percentile. Highest-leverage action: Business Model Resilience (79% materiality) — this single driver has the greatest impact on FTSE 100 ranking improvement.
summary_auditor: Of 4 ESG claims analysed, 0 (0%) are independently verifiable. 3 claim(s) directly contradicted by external data — high restatement risk. ESG provider spread: 19 pts — significant methodological disagreement reflected in Integrity Score. Priority: third-party audit of Business Model Resilience measurement methodology.
crowd_consensus: Aggregated signal across investor, corporate, and audit lenses: Lagging — underweight; high harm + ESG overstatement confirmed. Driver rankings are temporally unstable across market regimes (Jangani et al., SSRN 5618192). The 3-body instability — ESG score, peer rank, FTSE position pulling in different directions — is resolved by WEM (39/100) × Integrity (37/100): regime-stable signals grounded in verifiable harm, not rater methodology.
